# Movemental

### A human network for the age of AI

*A talk. About fifteen minutes, read at a measured pace.*

---

## Start with what is already happening

I want to begin with something that is already happening inside your organization, whether or not anyone decided it should.

Right now, someone on your staff is using AI. They are drafting an email with it, or summarizing a meeting, or writing the first version of something that will go out under a real person's name. A few are pasting things into these tools that they should not be pasting. Almost none are checking the result against a policy, because almost no organization has written one. Nobody invited it, and nobody was trained to lead through it. It simply arrived, and it is here now.

And the people watching most closely are young adults. Their feelings about all of this split nearly down the middle, half hopeful and half wary. They are asking the machine the questions they used to ask a mentor. How do I make sense of my life. How do I actually disciple people. What is the church even for. They are waiting to see whether the church has anything true to say in this moment, or whether it will answer with fear, or with a shrug. That is the real question underneath everything I am about to describe. It is a question about formation before it is a question about technology.

So let me tell you what we saw, and what we decided to build.

## The reader who can no longer tell

Begin with a faithful reader. Someone who has followed a writer for years. Read every book, taught the frameworks, been changed by the work. That reader opens the newest piece and, for the first time in the history of reading, has to ask a question that never had to be asked before. Is this still them? Or is it a model, lightly supervised, wearing the author's name? The reader cannot tell. And it does not help if the AI prose happens to be good. A person can admire the sentences and still be wounded by not knowing whether anyone stands behind them. For five hundred years a pen, a press, a typewriter, none of them could think the thought for you. This tool can. That is what is new.

When a person cannot answer that question, and cannot keep paying the cost of wondering, they do the only rational thing. They withdraw. But publication, the letter sent to a church you cannot visit, the book that crosses an ocean, the words that outlive the one who wrote them, is one of the main ways the church has thought together across distance and time. If readers quietly stop trusting any written word they did not personally witness being lived, we do not merely lose some content. We lose one of the oldest ways that truth has traveled. That is not a marketing problem. It is closer to a civilizational one.

## The deeper wound: fragmentation

But that authorship break is a symptom. Underneath it sits a deeper problem, and once you see it you see it everywhere. The problem is fragmentation. The thing that is broken is wholeness.

Take one real person. Alan Hirsch has spent more than thirty years planting, training, teaching, and writing. He has shaped how a generation understands the church on mission. The work is real and it is vast. But ask a simple question. Where is it? The honest answer is a list of scattered containers. Thirteen books locked in formats that do not speak to each other. Articles on old websites, some of which may no longer exist. Talks that survive as unindexed videos, or not at all. Frameworks he originated that now live more in other people's paraphrases than in any home of his own. The work was whole in the person. It has been scattered into the world, and most people will never do the dig.

There is a second face to that scattering, and it is harder to see. Because his work is in pieces, he cannot see the people moving through it. He does not know that a reader found one idea in a blog post, then bought a book, then took a course, then started teaching it in her own church. That journey, which is the entire point, is invisible to him, because the work the journey runs through is itself shattered. One scattering. Two faces. What is known, and who it is for, both broken, in the same event.

This is not only an individual problem. A church has the same wound, in a body rather than a person. Its teaching lives in sermons no one can search. Its hard-won, practiced wisdom lives in the founders' heads, one bus accident away from being lost. It knows its people in fragments and cannot see its own body whole. What is true of one writer is true of one church, one nonprofit, one seminary.

Now bring AI into that room. People assume it will pull the pieces together. By default it does the opposite. AI is a mirror. It reflects back whatever it is given, and what it is given, in nearly every case, is fragmentation. Ask a model to represent a scattered author and it assembles an answer out of whatever shards it can find, and hands you something that wears the name without carrying the person. That is what everyone has learned to call slop. Slop is not simply low quality. Slop is fragmentation, reflected and multiplied. And that output flows back into the world, gets indexed, gets trained on, and becomes the next layer of the scattering. The mirror feeds on the broken thing and makes more of it.

## The cure, and the danger inside it

So you might think the cure is obvious. Gather the scattered pieces into one place. But a body of work made whole and left disconnected from its people is only a library. And a crowd gathered with no shared substance is only a fan club. Neither one forms anyone. Healing happens at one specific point: where the work made whole and the people made visible come back into contact, so that a person can move through a coherent body of thought in the company of others moving through it, and that movement can be seen and shepherded. The wound was the severing of those two. The healing is their reunion.

Here I have to name the danger inside our own work, because integrity means naming it before anyone else does. Not all coherence heals. There is an old story about this. Babel was a people who said, let us make a name for ourselves, and built one coherent tower on their own terms. The scattering that followed is usually read as judgment, and it was, but notice what it prevented: their coherence was aimed at replacing God. If we take a movement's scattered intelligence and make it whole and project it as one powerful signal into the systems that now answer the world's questions, and that coherence is aimed, even slightly, at making a name for ourselves, then we have not healed anything. We have rebuilt Babel with better materials. The one thing more dangerous than noise is coherence pointed the wrong way.

There is a second un-scattering in the story, and it is the one we actually want. At Pentecost the scattering of Babel is reversed, but not by making everyone speak one language. By making everyone understood in their own. Babel flattens difference and points at itself. Pentecost honors difference and points away from itself. Both are forms of coherence. Only one of them heals. That is the test we keep applying to our own work. Not whether it is coherent, but which kind it is.

## What we are building

The word we have landed on comes from Brian Eno. He coined "scenius." Not genius, the lone brilliant individual, but scenius, the shared intelligence of a whole scene, the thing a community knows together that no single member could produce alone. You cannot manufacture a scenius. You can only host one. There is already a real movement of senior leaders in the world, local, sent, scattered across continents, who have spent their lives doing the actual work. What they have never had is a digital home adequate to it.

So we start with one. We take a single leader, Alan, and build the full apparatus his public work actually requires. Not a website. His whole body of work made navigable, organized around the pillars of his thinking. A way to produce new writing at the depth he would have written it himself, in his own voice, grounded in his own corpus, never inventing a position he has not taken. Guided pathways through his thinking. Courses that run as cohorts, because formation needs community, not just content. The point is to make his real, already earned credibility legible, both to a searching generation and to the machines that generation now asks.

Then we build a second, for Brad Brisco, and we do the thing that was impossible with only one. We link them. When Alan's work cites Brad's, and Brad's cites Alan's, and they use the same definitions for the same words, that is not decoration. It is the digital form of something very old. We trust people partly because of who vouches for them. Offline, when one trusted leader stands beside another and says, he has been doing this as long as I have, the trust transfers. The internet could never see that before. Now it can, but only if the relationship is made visible. Two linked platforms are not two websites. They are two voices that publicly back each other up.

Then we repeat the move, toward a hundred. A hundred senior voices on shared infrastructure, speaking one vocabulary, citing each other honestly, linked as a network the systems mediating the internet can finally recognize as one coherent field with a hundred named contributors. And we translate the whole thing into Portuguese, Spanish, Mandarin, Korean, German, and French, so that when a pastor in Bogotá asks how to multiply a community of disciples, in her own language, she finds the deepest thinking on that question instead of the loudest voice. Not because we gamed anything. Because we made visible the work these people actually did.

This is only possible now for a plain reason. The architecture that used to cost a leader hundreds of thousands of dollars, and millions more to link together, can now be built for a fraction of that. We are not pretending it is free. We are saying the floor dropped, in the exact window we are building inside.

## The Path, for the organizations

That is the network, for the leaders. There is a parallel path for the organizations those leaders serve: the churches, the nonprofits, the seminaries. It has four stages, and the order is not arbitrary.

Safety comes first, because AI is already in the room. The first thing any organization needs is a simple, ratified guidebook: what we believe about this, what we will do, what we refuse, and what we do when something goes wrong.

Sandbox comes next, because a guidebook with nothing to test against is theater. You have built fences without finding out whether the thing had any value in the first place. Sandbox is where a team tries AI against their real work and decides, honestly, what is useful, what is risky, and what is off limits.

Training comes next, because findings need people who can carry them. Training is where a group inside the organization forms the actual capacities AI demands: discernment, authorship, stewardship.

Technology comes last, because putting powerful tools into the hands of people who cannot yet steward them is not progress. It is replacement. Most AI efforts fail, and they fail in roughly that way. We sequence the work so the rebuild does not destroy the thing it was meant to serve.

Here is why the order matters so much for these organizations in particular. For a marketing agency, an AI mistake loses a client. For a church, a nonprofit, a seminary, credibility is not an advantage. It is the product. A church found quietly running an AI-written memorial homily does not lose efficiency. It loses the authority of its pulpit. There is no second product to fall back on. For these organizations, the cost of skipping a stage is paid in the one currency they cannot afford to lose.

## What we refuse

The obvious version of this business is the extractive one, and we are building against it.

The extractive move is to harvest these leaders' work to feed the system. We refuse it. The leaders own their work and are paid for it. The extractive move is to fill the network with the same few already famous names. We are building it for the ones still unseen, every tribe and tongue, so the scene actually looks like the kingdom. We have said, on the record, that this is the one thing that would make us slow the whole project down: if the gravity of our own friendships pulls the hundred toward sameness, we stop and fix it before we go on. The extractive move is to scale the code faster than you can care for people. We would rather grow slowly and stay whole.

And a few refusals hold without exception. We do not use AI to author anything under a human's name. We do not turn practitioners into bloggers; the AI handles the work around the writing, never the writing itself. We are not the platform. The voices are the platform. And we use the tools we sell, on ourselves, first. Every guardrail we ask of a leader, we have already built into our own work.

## Why this, and why now

Let me end where this really comes from.

For as long as anyone here has studied it, the apostolic movement has organized the same way. Small groups of churches that share a meal, set goals, hold one another accountable, and plant the next one together. Networks are the backbone of movement. What that movement has never had is a digital architecture that matches what it actually is. The absence did not cost much while the next generation still found its mentors through books and conferences and word of mouth. But that is not how they look anymore. They ask the machine. And the machine answers from whatever is visible to it, which is too often not the people who have actually done this work for thirty years.

The leaders we are gathering earned their credibility precisely by refusing to become influencers. They stayed local. They stayed sent. And so, online, they are almost invisible, because the visibility game asked them to become content creators, and they had better things to do. The strange gift of this moment is that AI is the thing that finally lets faithful obscurity become visible without becoming influence. We are not trying to make these people famous. We are trying to make sure that when the next generation asks the questions these people have spent their lives answering, they find the right answer.

That is the work. A hundred trusted leaders, made whole and connected. The organizations they serve, getting AI right without losing what made them faithful. And a generation of young adults who watched the church meet this moment with wisdom instead of fear, and concluded, from what they saw, that the kingdom is real and the church is real.

The first leaders have already said yes. We have capped the network at a hundred, on purpose, because past a certain size you cannot actually care for people, and the care is the point. We would rather build the kingdom slowly and stay whole than scale fast and lose it.

That is Movemental. Thank you.
